claims.claim: A new Malaysian party in the 12 months before an election is almost never trying to win the country — it is trying to win the negotiation the morning after the count.
claims.correction: Treat as an interpretive thesis unless supported by a coded dataset of party launches, vote share, seat outcome, coalition entry, and post-election bargaining role.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found that this is stale; the issue is lack of primary-source substantiation.

claims.claim: Sabah is the laboratory; the state has had eight ruling configurations in forty years. Sarawak's GPS is the stable counter-example because Sarawak's RM3-billion-a-year oil sales tax removes the incentive to fragment.
claims.correction: The Sarawak SST revenue point is broadly supportable, but the causal claim that SST removes the incentive to fragment is not established by primary sources. Sabah volatility is plausible but the 'eight ruling configurations in forty years' count needs a sourced definition.
The correct Act is Constitution (Amendment) (No. 3) Act 2022, Act A1663.

claims.claim: A Member ... shall cease to be a member ... on the date that the Member ceases to be a member of, or is expelled from, the political party for which he stood in the election.
claims.correction: The quoted wording is materially wrong. Article 49A creates vacancy when an MP resigns from the party or ceases to be a member, but Article 49A(2)(c) excludes expulsion from triggering vacancy.
Available legal-historical accounts indicate the 1962 amendment loosened the cap to roughly one-third deviation; the later 1973 amendment removed the quantified limit.

claims.claim: Today the largest parliamentary constituency contains roughly nine times as many voters as the smallest.
claims.correction: Using PRU15 EC figures, Bangi has 303,430 voters and Igan has 28,290 voters, implying about 10.7:1. The 9-10:1 figure appears to reflect older, pre-UNDI18 or 2018-era figures.
Parti Amanah Negara was launched/renamed in 2015, not 2017.

claims.claim: A 30-seat party in an 80-80-30 parliament does not hold 13 per cent of the power.
In GE15 November 2022, Pejuang contested 121 seats and won zero.
claims.correction: The 121-seat figure is reported for Gerakan Tanah Air, not cleanly for Pejuang alone. Reword to 'GTA, using Pejuang as its principal vehicle, contested 121 parliamentary seats and failed to win any.'

claims.claim: 56 of 222 federal seats held by Sabah + Sarawak (including Labuan FT).
claims.correction: Sabah has 25 parliamentary seats and Sarawak has 31, totalling 56. Including Labuan adds one Federal Territory seat, making 57 of 222.

claims.claim: The median first-contest result is roughly 3 per cent; five-year survival around 40 per cent; one in four launches end up in government.
claims.correction: This requires an underlying party-launch dataset, inclusion criteria, first-contest result table, survival definition, and coalition-entry coding. None is provided.

overall_assessment: The dossier's central analytical thesis is plausible, but its factual layer is not publication-ready. The most serious defects are the wrong anti-hopping Act number, incorrect Article 49A expulsion wording, stale voter-count/malapportionment figures, the Sabah-Sarawak-Labuan seat-count error, unsupported base-rate statistics, and overconfident causal framing around Sarawak SST. With corrections and citations, the piece could be salvageable, but as written it contains several high-risk legal and numerical errors.
